summary: Solid late‑80s set with strong Sugar > Sugaree and vibrant organ
blurb: The first set builds nicely from a lively Bertha into Desolation Row, while the second set launches with Sugar Magnolia into Sugaree and an inspired Estimated Prophet. Jerry’s solos stay sharp despite a broken string on Deal, and Brent’s Hammond organ adds vigor to the later jams.
review:
On September 11, 1987 at the Capital Centre, the Dead delivered a quintessential late‑80s performance that balances tight songcraft with moments of spontaneous joy. The first set is anchored by a buoyant Bertha that rolls into The Promised Land and a storytelling Desolation Row, though Deal suffers from a broken string and a slightly tired vocal delivery. The second set ignites the crowd as Sugar Magnolia segues straight into Sugaree, setting up an energetic Estimated Prophet that flows into Eyes of the World before the customary drums‑and‑space excursion. Brent Mydland’s organ shines on Dear Mr. Fantasy and All Along The Watchtower, adding a bright undercurrent to the jam. Mickey Hart’s birthday celebration injects extra excitement into the drumming, even if the percussion doesn’t break new ground. Overall, the show is solid with standout moments, especially the Sugar > Sugaree sequence, but recording inconsistencies and occasional mix issues keep it from reaching legendary status.

The audience tape “gd87-09-11.schoeps.dipietro.8906.sbeok.shnf” captures the full set with enough color to make up for its tinny character, making it the preferred listening source despite the technical flaws. Listeners looking for a representative snapshot of the Dead’s 1987 touring form will find this show worthwhile, while those seeking pristine audio may prefer to sample other dates from the same era.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The recordings show mixed sentiment overall: one clearly positive review, two mixed, and one negative. Positive remarks focus on strong set flow and standout songs (Sugaree, Sugar Magnolia), while the negative review notes severe technical issues. The balance yields a modest positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Full‑show audience tape with colorful ambience; despite tinny tone it captures the entire performance and highlights, making it the most complete and listenable source.

key_highlights: Strong Sugar Magnolia > Sugaree opener that drives the second set energy.
Brent Mydland’s Hammond organ adds vigor to Fantasy and Watchtower sections.
Mickey Hart’s birthday celebration injects extra excitement into the drums.
Recording quality varies, but the audience tape provides a complete picture of the show.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers strong solos throughout; a string broke near the end of Deal and his voice sounds a bit worn but still compelling.
band_performance.Phil: Often buried in the mix on audience recordings, though occasional harmonies with Brent cut through.
band_performance.Bob: Storytelling vocal performance shines on Desolation Row and carries solid energy across the set.
band_performance.Brent: Hammond organ comes alive toward the end, especially on Dear Mr. Fantasy and All Along The Watchtower, adding vigor to the jams.
band_performance.Mickey: Celebrates his birthday with extra excitement; drums/space segment is typical for the era but not a standout.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
